what_it_does: Analyzes a proposed mobile annotation gesture — long-press to arm, vertical thumb drag to change selection scale, release to commit — by validating it against named iOS interaction precedents and identifying a direct collision with native Safari long-press text selection, link/image previews, and vertical scrolling; it then prescribes concrete CSS and touch-event handling requirements to suppress native behavior while preserving normal scroll when the gesture is not armed.
observations: The gesture is classified as a press-and-drag-to-modulate-a-continuous-parameter interaction.
It compares the gesture to iOS keyboard spacebar trackpad mode, the text-selection loupe/magnifier, and Apple Pencil Pro squeeze.
It states that iOS long-press on text fires at about 0.5s and triggers native selection plus the Copy/Look Up/Share callout bar.
It notes that vertical drag is the deepest platform reflex because it maps to scrolling.
It proposes a custom long-press timer around 350–400ms so the arming gesture wins the race before Apple’s ~500ms callout.
It recommends setting -webkit-user-select: none and -webkit-touch-callout: none in annotation mode so the custom selector replaces native selection.
It emphasizes that touch-action/pointer suppression must apply only during the armed window and be restored afterward, because the page must still scroll normally when not armed.
It references Tim’s MSG2 instinct as an escape hatch and points forward to §3, which is not present in this file.
The file is a noticeboard block in channel dragnet-development and links to parent item-a5b7cf79.
standouts: The central argument that the proposed gesture collides head-on with iOS native long-press and scroll behaviors.
The specific prescription of a ~350–400ms custom timer to outrace iOS’s ~500ms native callout.
The nuanced requirement to disable touch-action only during the armed window rather than globally, so baseline scrolling remains intact.
conventions: Follows the noticeboard front-matter schema with id, address, type, source, state, title, links, and history.
Uses Markdown section headers and bold emphasis to mark claims and action items.
Cites shipping Apple interactions as design precedents.
Mixes high-level interaction rationale with low-level implementation details in one design note.
Uses internal cross-references like §3 without including that section in the same file.
concerns: The 0.5s and 350–400ms thresholds are asserted without cited measurement sources.
The concrete prescriptions assume a mobile Safari / webview implementation and do not discuss a native iOS app alternative.
References to MSG2 and §3 lack local context, so the escape hatch is not explained in this file alone.
It does not address accessibility interactions such as VoiceOver or Switch Control.
notes: A design-analysis note that validates a long-press-drag selection gesture against iOS native behavior and prescribes precise suppression timing to avoid collision with Safari selection and scroll.
questions: Is the target environment a mobile web app, a WKWebView wrapper, or a fully native iOS app?
What is MSG2 and what does Tim’s instinct specifically propose in §3?
Are the 350–400ms and 500ms thresholds based on measured data or heuristic estimates?
How should the gesture behave under VoiceOver, Switch Control, or other assistive technologies?
What is the content and status of the parent item-a5b7cf79?
purpose_vs_actual: The title frames a critique of a gesture idea, and the file both validates the instinct and pivots to concrete engineering constraints, matching its stated purpose.
apparent_themes: Suppressing native behavior without breaking baseline scrolling.
intention_signals: It is trying to prevent downstream implementers from underestimating the iOS platform collision in a proposed annotation gesture by reframing the design problem as a precise timing and event-handling contract.
novelty: The unusual combination of UX precedent validation, platform-collision forensics, and low-level CSS/touch-event prescriptions in a single design note.
contribution: It contributes a concrete platform-collision analysis and a specific event-handling contract — custom ~350–400ms timer, scoped touch-action suppression, and disabling user-select/touch-callout in annotation mode — that a fused system would keep when reasoning about the gesture’s feasibility on iOS Safari.
summary_for_embedding: This noticeboard item analyzes a proposed long-press-and-vertical-drag annotation gesture, validates it against iOS precedents, and details the native Safari collision risks and concrete CSS/touch-event suppression strategy required to make it work.
intention_for_embedding: It was filed to warn downstream implementers that the core gesture collides with iOS native selection and scroll and to specify the exact timing and event-handling contract needed to avoid failure.
